channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Even if Oil Prices Peak Soon, the Global Economy Will Slow This Year
item.description: The Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development, in a report, said the Middle East war’s consequences “are likely to be felt for some time.”
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil refinery in England. Higher energy prices because of the war in Iran will slow global economic growth and raise inflation through 2027, the O.E.C.D. said.

item.title: CBS News Fires Scott Pelley of ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: Mr. Pelley, a former “CBS Evening News” anchor, was ousted after months of tensions between staff and Bari Weiss, the network’s editor in chief.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:description: Scott Pelley was fired a day after a tense meeting where he accused the CBS editor in chief, Bari Weiss, of “murdering ‘60 Minutes.’”

item.title: NOTUS Cannot Be ‘The Star’: Judge Blocks D.C. News Outlet’s Rebranding
item.description: NOTUS, a political news website, must temporarily suspend its plans to rebrand, a judge ruled in a trademark dispute brought by The Washington Star.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: The original Washington Star, which was once called The Washington Star-News, was a conservative-leaning newspaper.

item.title: Colorado Enacts Law Tightening Scrutiny of Uber and Lyft
item.description: The law cites a New York Times investigation that found Uber received reports of sexual assault or sexual misconduct far more often than the company had publicly disclosed.
item.dc:creator: Emily Steel
item.media:description: Jenny Willford, the Colorado state representative who introduced the legislation.

item.title: White House Correspondents Dinner Rescheduled for July
item.description: The dinner was abruptly cut short in April when a gunman disrupted the proceedings. President Trump said that he would attend.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and the first lady, Melania Trump, at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner in April.

item.title: MSC Ship Is Struck by Two Projectiles in Iraqi Port
item.description: The attack on the vessel, owned by the Mediterranean Shipping Company, underlines the threat to ships even as the U.S. and Iran try to negotiate reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz. As part of the U.S. blockade against Iran, the United States has disabled five commercial vessels and redirected 121 as of Monday.

item.title: Stephen Curry and Li-Ning: What to Know About N.B.A. Star’s Deal With Chinese Sneaker Company
item.description: The agreement, which includes plans for sportswear and athleisure products, and stores across the United States and China, is the biggest involving an N.B.A. star and a Chinese brand.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:description: Stephen Curry of the Golden State Warriors during an April N.B.A. game.

item.title: New Proposal Would Allow Administration to Block Grants if They Don’t Support Trump’s Agenda
item.description: A new proposal would allow the administration to block grants if they do not satisfy President Trump’s agenda or support what it calls “anti-American” values.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:description: President Trump’s ambitions to impose new political criteria on grants were made clear in a roughly 400-page blueprint that was released on Friday.

item.title: Trump Megadonor Gave $5.5 Million Estate to Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s Anti-Vaccine Group
item.description: A Mellon heir transferred the adjacent Connecticut properties, covering about 300 acres, at no cost to Children’s Health Defense last year.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien and Theodore Schleifer
item.media:description: Timothy Mellon in 1981. During the 2024 election cycle, he donated $150 million to a Trump super PAC and $25 million to Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s super PAC.

item.title: Martin Scorsese Is Embracing A.I.
item.description: In a sign of Hollywood’s softening stance on artificial intelligence, the cinema icon is backing Black Forest Labs, an image and video generation start-up.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Philip Montgomery for The New York Times
item.media:description: Martin Scorsese signed on last year as a partner and an adviser to Black Forest Labs, a fast-rising A.I. venture.

item.title: What a Short-Seller’s Conviction Might Mean for Wall St.
item.description: A federal jury found Andrew Left, who rose to fame by betting against companies, guilty of securities fraud. Other short-sellers are worried.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Lauren Hirsch, Jonah E. Bromwich, Hurubie Meko and Ian Mount

item.title: What Ireland and Germany Can Teach Us About Birthright Citizenship
item.description: President Trump has argued that the United States is “stupid” for granting citizenship at birth. Most countries don’t do so, but that can create problems.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis, Megan Specia and Tatiana Firsova
item.media:credit: Ellius Grace for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mariam Sobayo was born in Dublin in February 2005, a few weeks after the country halted its longstanding practice of granting citizenship to all children born on Irish soil.

item.title: Glamour, a Once-Mighty Women’s Magazine, Turns to Shopping Links
item.description: Job cuts at Glamour and a greater focus on posts with shopping tips offer one of the clearest examples of the waning reign of women’s magazines.
item.dc:creator: Sapna Maheshwari
item.media:description: As part of the shift to a focus on affiliate shopping links, Condé Nast let go of much of Glamour’s remaining staff members last month and parted ways with its editor in chief, Samantha Barry.

item.title: Trump Targets Brazil With 25% Tariff, Citing Unfair Trade Practices
item.description: The proposal marks the latest effort by the administration to rebuild its tariff agenda through Section 301 investigations.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi
item.media:description: Bulk carriers at the port of Paranagua, Brazil, in March.

item.title: I.R.A. Rebates for Appliance Swaps Could Be Phased Out
item.description: New guidance from the Energy Department would prevent people from receiving rebates after making such swaps.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Bing Guan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new guidance would also end rebates for the replacement of gas-powered dryers, heat pumps and water heaters.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump as Middle East Tensions Build
item.description: Some analysts said the main international oil price, which was up 6 percent on Monday, could climb much higher in the coming weeks if the Strait of Hormuz doesn’t reopen.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Carson, Calif. The possibility of continued fighting has raised concerns about how long the world’s stockpiles of oil and fuels will last if the Strait of Hormuz does not reopen.

item.title: Scott Pelley Accuses CBS News Boss of ‘Murdering’ ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: In an explosive staff meeting, Mr. Pelley, a correspondent for the long-running Sunday news show, blasted Bari Weiss, the CBS editor in chief, and Nick Bilton, the show’s new executive producer.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum and Benjamin Mullin

item.title: Tribeca Festival 25th Anniversary: An Interview With Robert De Niro, Jane Rosenthal, Rebecca Glashow
item.description: Robert De Niro and Jane Rosenthal, who founded the event, and Rebecca Glashow, the new leader of Tribeca Enterprises, reflected on the festival’s legacy and ambitions.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Barry Diller’s People Inc. Plans a Takeover Bid for MGM Resorts
item.description: The mogul’s People Inc. is preparing an offer to buy the portion of the casino giant that it doesn’t already own, at an $18 billion valuation.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Vivienne Walt and Ian Mount
item.media:description: Barry Diller’s next big bet is on a giant of the Las Vegas Strip.

item.title: Chinese Military Sought Nvidia Chips for Years, Report Says
item.description: An analysis of six years of procurement records suggests that the People’s Liberation Army has openly tried to acquire restricted U.S. technology.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Tripp Mickle
item.media:description: China’s military has stepped up its efforts to acquire artificial intelligence chips despite U.S. restrictions, according to Wirescreen, a software platform.

item.title: Prized by ‘MAHA’ Influencers and Chefs Alike, Craft Flour Is on the Rise
item.description: Growing demand for fiber-rich grains is boosting the premium flour industry, which offers less processed, but pricier, alternatives to the classic grocery store brands.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Kronsberg
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Morse, left, C.E.O. and co-founder of Cairnspring Mills, with Bryan Ford at Ford’s Brooklyn bakery, Diljān.

item.title: Oil Prices Spike as U.S. and Iran Exchange Fire
item.description: Oil prices climbed as investors weighed a renewed exchange of military strikes between the United States and Iran against signs that both sides remain engaged in negotiations.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: He Became China’s Largest Critic in Exile. Then the Threats Followed Him.
item.description: Li Ying, known as Teacher Li to his 2.2 million followers on X, doesn’t live in China but still faces smear campaigns and death threats. He is not letting that stop him.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: U.S. Military Is Quietly Guiding Ships Through the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: U.S. Central Command has helped around 70 commercial ships pass through the strait in the last three weeks, an official said.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels waiting to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Before the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran, well over 100 commercial ships a day passed through the strait.

item.title: Young Moviegoers Power ‘Backrooms’ to $82 Million in Ticket Sales
item.description: The horror film, which cost $10 million and came from a 20-year-old filmmaker, added to evidence that young people will go to theaters for the right offering.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: Can the World Cup Help the U.S. Beer Industry Kick Its Slump?
item.description: Bars and stores are uncertain if the soccer tournament will boost beer sales. Still, the maker of Budweiser and Michelob Ultra is going all in.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Total Wine & More store in River Edge, N.J. MetLife Stadium in nearby East Rutherford will host the World Cup final.

item.title: What Is Airbnb For, Exactly?
item.description: The home-sharing app recently added car rentals, groceries and — perhaps most surprisingly — hotels. Brian Chesky, the co-founder and chief executive, is always thinking about how to redesign it.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Chesky helped popularize the term “founder mode” in 2024, after feeling like Airbnb was getting too bogged down by bureaucracy.

item.title: Is This the Future of Air Travel?
item.description: A new all-electric aircraft made a test flight in New York City. Our transportation reporter Niraj Chokshi describes what to know.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi, Sutton Raphael and James Surdam

item.title: Joby Demonstrated its Air Taxi in Manhattan, but You Can’t Fly in It Yet
item.description: Aviation start-ups and the Trump administration want to replace helicopters with electric aircraft, but the new vehicles still have to pass arduous tests before the public can use them.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi

item.title: Dang Van Phuoc Dies at 90; Intrepid Photojournalist in Vietnam War
item.description: A photographer for The Associated Press, he spent a decade on the front lines of combat in his native Vietnam and lost his right eye in a grenade explosion.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: When War Breaks Out Over the Family Business, They Get the Call
item.description: As baby boomers begin passing down trillions in assets, advisers who specialize in navigating the process — and the emotions around it — have never been busier.
item.dc:creator: Brent Crane
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Doug Baumoel founded Continuity Family Business Consulting, in Massachusetts, to “help families prevent, manage and heal from conflict over business and money.”

item.title: How Your Friend Group Influences How You Spend and Save
item.description: Our spending habits are often influenced by our social circles. You can help shape what it looks like.
item.dc:creator: Paulette Perhach
item.media:credit: Benjamin Norman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sandy Smith said she embraced being “cheap” because “I had to.” Now her friends come to her for financial advice.

item.title: Making Motherhood and a Long Tennis Career Possible
item.description: Tennis is a solitary sport, but with new fertility benefits, a sisterhood of egg freezers is emerging in locker rooms and players’ lounges.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
item.media:credit: Matthew Avignone for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I have found this absolutely newfound admiration for women that go through those cycles over and over,” Magda Linette said.

item.title: China’s Rise in Drug Development Looms Over U.S.
item.description: Clinical trials in China are getting attention at an international oncology gathering in Chicago. China’s surging biotechnology industry is fueling alarm that U.S. dominance in the field is waning.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins and Gina Kolata
item.media:description: The American Society of Clinical Oncology annual meeting in Chicago last year. One of the conference’s coveted headliners this year is a presentation of a clinical trial conducted only in China.

item.title: S.E.C. Proposes to Kill Climate Change Disclosure Rule
item.description: The regulation would have required all publicly traded companies to disclose whether they faced significant risks from climate change and its effects.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil drilling site in Wyoming. The S.E.C. rule would have required companies to disclose their contributions to climate change and the risks they faced from a warming planet.

item.title: For Wall Street, the Only Thing Worse Than SpaceX Flopping Is Missing Out
item.description: Elon Musk and his bankers are working to create a self-fulfilling prophecy for the massive public offering: Make investors feel that the risks of passing it up outweigh those of buying into it.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Farrell and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: SpaceX’s launch site near Boca Chica, Texas, in 2024. The company could go public as soon as next month.

item.title: Sky-High I.P.O. Pricing Isn’t Great for Real People
item.description: When newly public companies have been valued as richly as SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic seem likely to be, the outlook for ordinary investors has been poor.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.media:description: SpaceX’s Starship rocket during a test flight in May. The company’s chief executive, Elon Musk, stands to unlock a great fortune when the company goes public.

item.title: Inside the Room Where War Insurance Is Bought and Sold
item.description: Many of the ships stranded in the Persian Gulf depend on coverage negotiated at Lloyd’s, the center of marine insurance for more than 300 years.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 200-foot-tall atrium in the Underwriting Room, where brokers negotiate policies for ships carrying oil, gas and other goods around the world.

item.title: Trump Clears Way for Companies to Avoid Taxes in Havens Including Malta and Cyprus
item.description: U.S. companies skirted at least $40 billion in taxes since the beginning of 2025 thanks to schemes in places like Malta, Bermuda and Cyprus.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Drucker and Dylan Freedman
item.media:credit: Susan Wright for The New York Times
item.media:description: A favorite destination for tax-dodging by U.S. companies was the tiny Mediterranean island of Malta.

item.title: The $900 Billion Giant: How Anthropic Got So Big, So Fast
item.description: The artificial intelligence giant was just valued at $900 billion, surpassing OpenAI. Here are the numbers behind its rise — and headwinds it faces.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic, which created the Claude A.I. model, has surged in popularity with users, pushing its valuation to $900 billion.

item.title: Luka Doncic on Trying to Bring an N.B.A. Team to Rome
item.description: The superstar is part of group that has bought a basketball team in Italy, with a plan to move the club to Rome in hopes of making it part of a new league.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
item.media:description: “Since I came to the N.B.A., my dream was always to own a team in Europe,” said Luka Doncic, a Slovenian basketball star who plays for the Los Angeles Lakers.

item.title: Providence Isn’t an Official World Cup Host, but It Sure Is Acting Like One
item.description: A stadium outside Boston that will host seven World Cup games is actually closer to Providence, R.I., prompting officials there to capitalize on that proximity to lure fans.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Kieran Dodds for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans of the Scottish soccer team in Glasgow in 2021. Fans of Scotland’s World Cup team are expected to fill accommodations in Providence, R.I., to watch matches at a Massachusetts stadium next month.

item.title: A.I. Doesn’t Have to Mean Layoffs
item.description: A French multinational, Schneider Electric, decided to use artificial intelligence in manufacturing to make workers more productive, rather than to replace them. Here’s how that’s going.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen and Alexis Pazoumian

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ Is Part of a Boomlet in Movies From YouTube Creators
item.description: “Backrooms,” a psychological horror flick opening this weekend, is part of a wave of breakout films from fledgling directors who honed their instincts on YouTube.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:description: The director of “Backrooms,” Kane Parsons, left, on a set with one of the new film’s stars, Chiwetel Ejiofor.

item.title: How NextEra Energy Wielded Political Power in Florida
item.description: NextEra, which is seeking to buy Dominion Energy, has often butted heads with consumer groups, residents and journalists in its home state.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:description: A Dominion Energy power plant near Davis, W.Va. NextEra Energy’s political activities are expected to be scrutinized as it seeks approval of its purchase of Dominion.

item.title: How to Travel if You Have Panic Attacks
item.description: You don’t have to let severe anxiety keep you from exploring the world. Here’s how to manage your panic on vacation.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Lauretta

item.title: Oil Prices Fall as U.S. and Iran Edge Closer to Agreement
item.description: Oil prices and stock markets fluctuated as investors assessed tentative signs of a pact to extend a cease-fire.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Europe Is Edging Closer to a Trade War With China. Here’s Why.
item.description: As cheap goods pour in, threatening the continent’s manufacturing sector, a search for solutions is becoming increasingly urgent.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Alexandra Stevenson
item.media:description: Chinese-made electric vehicles awaiting export at the port in Hangzhou, eastern China, in May.

item.title: ABC Accuses Trump Administration of Threatening Its Broadcast Licenses
item.description: The TV network slammed the Federal Communications Commission, saying the regulator was targeting its station licenses in a campaign of “unconstitutional retaliation.”
item.dc:creator: Jim Rutenberg
item.media:description: The filing was the second time this month that ABC accused the Federal Communications Commission of violating its free speech rights because of what the network has called “speech the government does not like.”
